summary: Hotz defends his prediction that Tesla is 8 years away from fully solving self-driving, while maintaining Tesla will solve it first. He dismisses critics as politically-minded stock shills rather than people with genuine technical understanding. He defines 'solved' as driving on par with skilled humans in every scenario, not merely being useful. He draws a parallel to AI coding—useful but not yet human-level—and embraces his role as someone whose nuanced takes anger partisans on all sides.
key_points: Tesla FSD is 8 years from being truly finished, meaning it drives as well as skilled humans in every scenario with zero mistakes
Being useful (like comma.ai already is) is different from being finished—the bar is repeatedly betting your life on cross-country drives
AI coding assistants are better than beginners but not yet better than skilled humans, similar to how minimax beats novices at chess
Critics mistake anecdotal success (1,000 miles of self-driving) for technical understanding
Nuanced opinions that don't fit tribal narratives anger people on all sides
money_quotes: At least FSD is real AI that you can evalulate the honest progress of, unlike remote control cars that break when the power goes out.
I'm constantly trying the latest stuff, I got a ride in a Cybertruck from JFK to downtown Manhattan two days ago (one intervention).
That you'd repeatedly bet your life on it driving across the country without making a single mistake.
AI's will eventually become better programmers than humans, they just aren't yet.
I like AI, I want it to be good, it will someday be good, and right now it's kind of good, but it's not that good yet.
But commenters seem to be dumb politically minded shills who need to sort every comment into with us or against us.
I'm against all of you and with the truth.
key_insight: Nuanced technical assessments that don't fit neat tribal narratives—acknowledging something is promising while noting its current limitations—will anger partisans who demand you pick a side.
